race.name: L'Etape Argentina by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Etape Argentina by Tour de France
race.tagline: ASO's Tour de France franchise returns to Argentina after an 11-year absence -- 134 km through the Sierras de Cordoba with the Argentine National Road Championship running alongside.

vitals.location: Cordoba, Cordoba Province, Argentina (Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes start/finish)
vitals.entry_fee: Estimated ~$80-150 USD per entry (typical L'Etape franchise pricing for South American market)
vitals.event_date: March 22, 2026 (inaugural Cordoba edition; event previously held in Argentina ~2014-2015)
distance_options: The Race (Long Course): 134 km, 2,067 m elevation gain
The Ride (Short Course): 64 km, moderate elevation gain
terrain_types: Mix of highway and rural roads
vitals.registration: Online via argentina.letapeseries.com. Each cyclist receives timing chip, race number, access wristband, insurance, hydration, food, medical, mechanical, and sweep services.
vitals.prize_purse: Tour de France classification jerseys. No cash prizes for amateur participants.
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple feed zones with water, energy drinks, and food. Medical assistance and mechanical support on course. Sweep vehicle service.
vitals.cutoff_time: Broom wagon enforced. Riders behind time cutoffs are pulled from course.

climate.primary: Late Argentine summer / early autumn (March) at moderate elevation in the Sierras de Cordoba
climate.description: March marks the transition from summer to autumn in Cordoba. The city sits at approximately 400m elevation with a subtropical climate. Daytime temperatures average 20-27C (68-81F), dropping to 13-16C (55-61F) at night. March receives moderate rainfall (~100mm across the month) with occasional afternoon thunderstorms typical of the late summer pattern. Humidity is moderate. The Sierras de Cordoba provide slightly cooler conditions at altitude, with the Camino del Cuadrado section (~1,200m) offering refreshing mountain air. Conditions are generally favorable for road cycling -- warm but not oppressively hot, with manageable humidity and a low probability of sustained rain on any given day.
challenges: Afternoon thunderstorms possible -- typical late-summer weather pattern for Cordoba region
Warm temperatures (25-27C) at lower elevations can cause dehydration if fueling is neglected
Sun exposure over 5-7 hours of riding requires thorough sunscreen application
Temperature differential between valley floor (27C) and mountain sections (18-20C) requires awareness

terrain.primary: Paved mountain and valley roads through the Sierras de Cordoba and Punilla Valley
terrain.surface: 100% sealed roads through Cordoba province. The route uses a combination of the Cordoba-Carlos Paz highway, the new Autovia Punilla, the scenic Camino del Cuadrado mountain pass, and Route E53. Road surface quality ranges from excellent on the newer Autovia sections to good on the mountain pass roads. The Camino del Cuadrado was recently repaved and features sweeping curves with mountain views. Fully closed to traffic on race day.
features: Fully closed roads -- Tour de France race conditions
Start and finish at iconic Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes
Camino del Cuadrado -- signature mountain pass climbing from Punilla Valley
Punilla Valley roads through sierras, valleys, and rivers
Two distance options: 134 km (The Race) and 64 km (The Ride)
Tour de France classification jerseys and branding
Medical, mechanical, and sweep support on course
Argentine National Road Championship running concurrently (separate elite event)

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: ASO's Tour de France franchise event returning to Argentina after an 11-year hiatus, now hosted in Cordoba. The 134 km route through the Sierras de Cordoba and Punilla Valley delivers 2,067m of climbing. Co-location with the Argentine National Road Championship (returning after 25 years) adds significance to the 2026 edition. First modern edition means limited proven track record, but ASO franchise standards and 3,000+ expected riders signal serious intent.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: logistics=3: ASO-standard organization at Kempes Stadium, Cordoba has its own international airport but secondary to Buenos Aires. length=3: 134 km is solid but not epic. technicality=1: 100% paved roads. elevation=3: 2,067m is respectable but climbing density (15.4m/km) is moderate. climate=2: warm March weather is favorable, not a major challenge. altitude=1: peaks at ~1,280m, no altitude factor. adventure=3: Punilla Valley scenery is attractive but fully supported. prestige=3: Tour de France brand with national championship co-location. race_quality=3: ASO franchise standards expected but unproven in this configuration. experience=3: TdF atmosphere at iconic stadium, but first modern edition. community=3: Argentine cycling is passionate but event community is being rebuilt. field_depth=2: recreational field, domestic participants. value=3: reasonable pricing with full support. expenses=4: Argentina is very affordable for international visitors. cultural_impact=2: return after 11 years with national championship adds significance. (37 base + 2 cultural) / 70 = 56.

logistics.explanation: The event infrastructure is promising: Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes is an iconic venue (1978 World Cup final, 57,000 capacity) providing a memorable start/finish location with ample staging area. ASO franchise standards mean professional timing, medical teams, mechanical support, feed zones, and sweep wagons. Cordoba has its own international airport (Ingeniero Aeronautico Ambrosio L.V. Taravella, COR) with direct flights from Buenos Aires, Lima, Santiago, and Panama City -- eliminating the need to transit through Buenos Aires by ground. However, this is a relaunch edition after 11 years, so the local organizing team is unproven in this specific configuration. The route uses a mix of highways and mountain roads that require significant road-closure coordination. For international visitors outside South America, reaching Cordoba requires a connection through Buenos Aires or another regional hub.

length.explanation: 134 km (83 miles) with 2,067m of climbing is a meaningful day on the bike. The climbing density of approximately 15.4 meters per kilometer is moderate -- demanding enough to require specific preparation but not in the same crushing category as Alpine or Andean events. The 64 km short course makes the event accessible to intermediate riders who want the Tour de France atmosphere without the full endurance test. Most long-course finishers should complete in 5-8 hours depending on fitness. It is a proper sportive distance, but it does not approach the epic scale of the French L'Etape original (170+ km, 5,000+ meters of climbing) or the Gran Fondo monuments.

technicality.explanation: This is road cycling on sealed Argentine roads. Zero technical terrain. The Camino del Cuadrado features winding mountain road with curves, but the road surface is newly paved and the challenge is cardiovascular, not technical. The Autovia Punilla sections are smooth divided highway. Descents require standard road bike handling skills. Bring a road bike with a compact crank and appropriate gearing for the climbs. Tire choice is irrelevant beyond standard road rubber.

elevation.explanation: 2,067 meters of climbing in 134 km is respectable. The route climbs from the Cordoba valley floor (~400m) up through the Sierras de Cordoba, with the Camino del Cuadrado as the signature ascent taking riders to approximately 1,280m before descending through the Punilla Valley. The climbing is concentrated in distinct blocks rather than evenly distributed, meaning there are recovery sections between the major efforts. For an average recreational rider, the accumulated vertical is genuinely challenging and requires climbing-specific training. But this is not the relentless climbing density of the Brazilian L'Etape (2,300m in 107 km) or European mountain sportives. The profile described as evoking a 'Tour de France mountain stage' is marketing-forward -- it is hilly with a proper mountain pass, but not a true HC stage equivalent.

climate.explanation: Late March in Cordoba is climatically favorable for road cycling. The worst of summer heat has passed, with temperatures settling into a comfortable 20-27C range. Humidity is moderate, not the oppressive subtropical moisture of Buenos Aires or coastal Brazil. The Sierras provide cooler air at altitude. The main climate risk is afternoon thunderstorms, which are common in this transitional season -- but the early morning start should have most riders finished or nearly finished before afternoon convection develops. Climate is not a defining challenge of this event. It scores low because the weather is essentially pleasant rather than punishing.

altitude.explanation: Cordoba city sits at approximately 400m above sea level. The highest point on the course, the Camino del Cuadrado, reaches approximately 1,280m (4,200 ft). This is genuinely low altitude -- well below any threshold where altitude physiology affects performance. No rider, regardless of origin, needs acclimatization for this event. The altitude score reflects the absence of this challenge dimension entirely.

adventure.explanation: The Sierras de Cordoba offer genuine scenic value: granite mountains, river valleys, small towns with their own character, and the sweeping views from the Camino del Cuadrado across the Punilla Valley. Cordoba province's landscape diversity -- sierras, valleys, rivers, and traditional towns -- creates an attractive backdrop for cycling. The sense of riding through the 'heart of Argentina' rather than the cosmopolitan Buenos Aires gives the event a more authentic regional character. However, this is a fully supported road event with feed zones and sweep wagons. The adventure comes from the destination and scenery rather than remoteness or self-reliance. For non-Argentine riders, the cultural adventure of cycling in Argentina's interior adds genuine novelty.

prestige.explanation: The Tour de France brand is the most recognized name in professional cycling, and the L'Etape franchise transfers meaningful prestige to its satellite events. The co-location of the Argentine National Road Championship (returning to Cordoba after 25 years) elevates the weekend's significance beyond a typical franchise event. But this is a relaunch after an 11-year absence -- the event needs to re-establish itself. It is one of 30+ L'Etape events worldwide, and the Argentine edition does not yet carry the established reputation of the French original, Brazilian franchise (10+ years running), or the Australian edition. Within Argentine cycling, the 2026 edition will be significant. Globally, it is a new data point.

race_quality.explanation: ASO franchise events reliably deliver professional organization: chip timing, classification jerseys, medical teams, mechanical support, feed zones, and a proper finish-line experience. The provincial government of Cordoba is actively involved, adding institutional support and road-closure authority. Each participant receives a timing chip, race number, insurance coverage, and full on-course support services. However, this is the first modern edition -- ASO franchise standards are the expectation, but execution is unproven for this specific route and organizing team. The Brazilian franchise ran flawlessly after 10 years of refinement; the Argentine edition is starting from scratch. A score of 3 reflects high expectation tempered by zero track record.

experience.explanation: Starting and finishing at the iconic Kempes Stadium -- site of the 1978 World Cup final -- is a genuinely memorable setting. The Tour de France branding, classification jerseys, and event village create the familiar L'Etape atmosphere. The Punilla Valley scenery through the Sierras de Cordoba provides a photogenic course. Preliminary activities on March 20-21 include kit pickup and brand activations. The concurrent Argentine National Championship adds spectacle for cycling fans. But as a first modern edition, the experience infrastructure is being built for the first time. The community traditions, local knowledge, and refined logistics that come from multi-year events do not yet exist. Expect a solid but not yet polished experience.

community.explanation: Argentine cycling culture is passionate and growing. The country has a professional road cycling tradition (Vuelta a San Juan draws WorldTour teams), and recreational cycling is booming in Cordoba province. 3,000+ expected participants for a relaunch event demonstrates genuine demand. The national championship co-location will bring the Argentine cycling community's elite and enthusiast layers together for a historic weekend. However, L'Etape Argentina as a specific community does not yet exist in its modern form -- the 11-year gap means the previous participant base has dispersed. The community score reflects the strong foundation of Argentine cycling culture rather than an established event-specific community.

field_depth.explanation: The field will be overwhelmingly recreational and club-level Argentine riders pursuing a personal challenge with Tour de France branding. Argentina has strong domestic road cyclists -- riders from the Vuelta a San Juan feeder system and active club scene -- but the L'Etape amateur field will not feature meaningful international competitive depth. The concurrent national championship is a separate elite event. Within the L'Etape field, the front of the race will feature strong regional riders, but overall competitive depth is thin by European sportive standards.

value.explanation: At typical L'Etape franchise pricing (~$80-150 USD range for the South American market), the entry fee is reasonable for the included support: timing chip, race number, insurance, hydration, food, medical, mechanical, and sweep services on course, plus the Tour de France branding and classification jersey experience. The value proposition is solid for domestic Argentine riders. For international visitors, the event provides an affordable entry point into the L'Etape franchise compared to the European editions (EUR175+ for France). The short course option adds accessibility for those wanting the atmosphere without the full commitment.

expenses.explanation: Argentina's economic reality makes it exceptionally affordable for international visitors. The Argentine peso's weakness against the USD, EUR, and GBP means accommodation, food, and ground transportation are a fraction of European or North American prices. Cordoba has abundant accommodation from budget hotels to comfortable options at $30-80 USD per night. Argentine food -- asado, empanadas, wine -- is world-class and remarkably cheap. For domestic Argentine riders, costs are moderate by local standards. A full race weekend (entry, accommodation, food, travel within Argentina) might cost $200-400 USD for international visitors -- a fraction of what a European L'Etape weekend demands.

biased_opinion.verdict: The Tour de France Returns to the Pampas (Via the Sierras)
biased_opinion.summary: L'Etape Argentina brings the Tour de France franchise back to South America's second-largest country after an 11-year hiatus, and the choice of Cordoba over Buenos Aires signals ambition. The 134 km route through the Sierras de Cordoba and the scenic Punilla Valley delivers 2,067m of climbing anchored by the Camino del Cuadrado -- a proper mountain pass that earns its place in the route profile. Starting and finishing at Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes adds genuine gravitas. The co-location with the Argentine National Road Championship returning after a 25-year absence transforms this from a standard franchise event into a historically significant cycling weekend. Argentina's affordability makes this one of the best-value L'Etape events globally. The limitation is the obvious one: this is a relaunch, not an established event. ASO franchise standards set the expectation high, but the local organizing team has zero modern track record. The competitive field will be thin by international standards. And while the Sierras de Cordoba are scenic, this is not the Andes -- the climbing, altitude, and scale do not match what Argentina's western mountains could theoretically offer.
strengths: Tour de France franchise branding with ASO organizational standards
Iconic start/finish at Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes (1978 World Cup venue)
Camino del Cuadrado provides a legitimate mountain pass climbing challenge
Argentine National Road Championship co-location adds historic significance
Exceptional value -- Argentina's exchange rate makes this one of the cheapest L'Etape events globally
Cordoba has its own international airport, avoiding Buenos Aires transit
3,000+ expected riders for a relaunch demonstrates strong domestic demand
Punilla Valley scenery through sierras, valleys, rivers, and traditional towns
weaknesses: Relaunch after 11-year absence -- zero modern track record to evaluate
134 km with 2,067m of climbing is respectable but not elite-tier mountain course
Thin international competitive field -- overwhelmingly domestic participation expected
First edition logistics risk -- route coordination, road closures, and support are unproven
Low altitude (~1,280m peak) means no meaningful altitude challenge dimension
Limited global visibility compared to established L'Etape franchise events in France, Brazil, or Australia
Afternoon thunderstorm risk in late March Cordoba
biased_opinion.bottom_line: L'Etape Argentina 2026 is a bet on potential. The ingredients are there: the Tour de France brand, a scenic mountain route, an iconic venue, and the added weight of the Argentine National Championship returning to Cordoba after 25 years. Argentina's affordability makes this the best-value L'Etape franchise event you can find. But this is a first modern edition with an unproven organizing team on a route that has never been raced in this configuration. If ASO's franchise quality control delivers and the local team executes, this could become a standout South American cycling event within 2-3 editions. For 2026, it is worth the trip if you are in South America, a L'Etape completionist, or want a destination cycling experience at a fraction of European pricing. It is not yet worth a transatlantic trip on its own merits.

final_verdict.one_liner: ASO's Tour de France franchise relaunches in Argentina after 11 years: 134 km through the Sierras de Cordoba with 2,067m of climbing, starting from the iconic Kempes Stadium alongside the returning Argentine National Road Championship.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you are based in South America and want a professionally organized Tour de France-branded sportive at exceptional value. Yes if the historic co-location with the Argentine National Championship appeals. Yes if you want to be part of a relaunch edition that could become significant. Skip if you want a proven event with multi-year track record -- this is edition one in its modern form. Skip if you need deep international competition or extreme mountain climbing. Skip if you are traveling from Europe or North America solely for this event -- the Brazilian franchise is more established.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the definitive L'Etape experience: L'Etape du Tour de France (France, July). For an established L'Etape in South America: L'Etape Brazil (Campos do Jordao, September). For road cycling in Argentina with professional-level competition: Vuelta a San Juan spectator experience. For affordable South American cycling: GFNY Colombia.

logistics.airport: Ingeniero Aeronautico Ambrosio L.V. Taravella International Airport (COR) -- Cordoba's own international airport, 11 km from city center and Kempes Stadium. Direct flights from Buenos Aires (1.5 hrs), Lima, Santiago, Panama City, and seasonal international connections.
logistics.alternate_airports: Buenos Aires Ezeiza (EZE) -- Argentina's main international hub, 700 km / 8 hours drive or 1.5 hour domestic flight to Cordoba. Buenos Aires Aeroparque (AEP) -- domestic flights to Cordoba, centrally located in Buenos Aires.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Cordoba is Argentina's second-largest city with abundant accommodation at all price points. The Nueva Cordoba and Centro neighborhoods offer the most options within easy reach of Kempes Stadium. Budget hotels and hostels from $20-40 USD/night; comfortable mid-range hotels $50-100 USD/night. Airbnb options are plentiful and affordable. Book 1-2 months in advance for race weekend. The Punilla Valley towns (La Falda, Villa Carlos Paz, Cosquin) offer scenic alternatives 30-60 minutes from the start.
logistics.food: Cordoba is a major Argentine city with excellent dining. Asado (Argentine barbecue), empanadas cordobesas (Cordoba's signature deep-fried empanadas), locro, and world-class Malbec wine. Carb-loading is effortless in Argentina -- pasta, bread, and medialunas (Argentine croissants) are abundant and cheap. Post-race asado celebration is practically mandatory. Feed zones on course stocked with water, energy drinks, and food.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village at Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes, March 20-21 (Friday-Saturday before race day). Registration, kit collection, and brand activations.
logistics.parking: Kempes Stadium has substantial parking infrastructure (built for 57,000-capacity football matches). Additional designated event parking areas expected. Arrive early on race morning.
logistics.travel_summary: Fly into Cordoba (COR) directly -- the airport is 20 minutes from the start venue. Alternatively, fly into Buenos Aires and connect via a short domestic flight (1.5 hours) or long-distance bus (9-10 hours). Cordoba is well-connected by Argentina's domestic transport network. Within the city, taxis and ride-shares are extremely affordable. No car rental needed unless you plan to explore the Sierras de Cordoba region post-race.

history.founder: Local organizing partner under license from ASO (Amaury Sport Organisation -- Tour de France organizer), with support from the Government of Cordoba Province
history.origin_story: L'Etape Argentina was originally part of ASO's early expansion of the L'Etape franchise to South America, with editions held in Argentina around 2014-2015. The event went dormant, and the franchise's South American presence shifted to Brazil, where L'Etape Brasil became the continent's flagship amateur cycling event from 2015 onward. After an 11-year absence, ASO announced the return of L'Etape Argentina for 2026, this time in the cycling-friendly province of Cordoba rather than Buenos Aires. The choice of Cordoba reflects the province's investment in sports tourism and cycling infrastructure, as well as its Sierras providing genuine mountain terrain. The Government of Cordoba Province has been actively involved in the organization, positioning the event as a centerpiece of regional sports tourism. The concurrent return of the Argentine National Road Championship to Cordoba after 25 years -- organized by UCRA (Union Ciclista de la Republica Argentina) alongside FACPyR and local entities -- transforms the March 2026 weekend into the most significant cycling event in Argentine history in a generation.
notable_moments: ~2014-2015: Original L'Etape Argentina editions held (details limited from dormant period)
2015-2025: L'Etape franchise in South America concentrated in Brazil (L'Etape Brasil runs continuously)
2025: ASO announces return of L'Etape Argentina for 2026, selecting Cordoba as host city
2026: Government of Cordoba Province opens registrations; 3,000+ riders expected
2026: Argentine National Road Championship returns to Cordoba after 25-year absence, running alongside L'Etape
March 20-21, 2026: Event village and kit collection at Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes
March 22, 2026: Race day -- 134 km and 64 km routes through the Sierras de Cordoba and Punilla Valley
history.reputation: As a relaunch event, L'Etape Argentina does not yet have an established modern reputation. The Tour de France brand provides immediate credibility, and the Government of Cordoba's institutional backing adds legitimacy. The co-location with the returning Argentine National Road Championship creates a historically significant cycling weekend. Within the L'Etape franchise ecosystem (30+ events in 21 countries), Argentina represents a new frontier in a cycling-mad but underserved market. The 2026 edition will define the event's reputation going forward.

course_description.character: A road course through the Sierras de Cordoba that climbs from the city's valley floor into the Punilla Valley mountain region. The 134 km long course starts and finishes at Estadio Mario Alberto Kempes (~400m elevation), heads north through the Sierras Chicas to Rio Ceballos, climbs the Camino del Cuadrado to approximately 1,280m, then descends through the Punilla Valley past Valle Hermoso before returning via the Autovia Punilla and Cordoba-Carlos Paz highway. The route character alternates between fast highway sections and winding mountain roads, with the Camino del Cuadrado serving as the decisive climbing challenge. The scenery transitions from suburban Cordoba through forested foothills to open mountain vistas across the Punilla Valley.

suffering_zones.label: Kempes Stadium Departure
suffering_zones.desc: Early morning rollout from the iconic stadium. The first kilometers head north through suburban Cordoba on the approach to the Sierras Chicas. The field sorts itself on the gently rising terrain. March morning temperatures are comfortable (18-20C at start). Nervous energy and the Tour de France atmosphere keep the pace honest from the gun.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Paved urban and suburban roads, gentle gradient northward toward Rio Ceballos

suffering_zones.label: Camino del Cuadrado Ascent
suffering_zones.desc: The signature climb of the course. The road rises steeply from the Rio Ceballos side, gaining approximately 800m over a concentrated distance. Gradients reach 8-12% on the steepest pitches. Sweeping curves reveal increasingly dramatic views of the Punilla Valley below. This is where the race is decided -- the climbing separates those who trained specifically from those who hoped general fitness would carry them. The recently repaved surface is smooth, but the gradient is relentless. The KOM classification is contested here.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Mountain pass road, steep gradients, switchbacks, 1,280m peak elevation

suffering_zones.label: Punilla Valley Descent and Rollers
suffering_zones.desc: The descent from Camino del Cuadrado drops into the Punilla Valley -- a sweeping downhill that rewards confident descenders and punishes those who brake through every curve. The valley floor provides brief respite on rolling terrain through Valle Hermoso and surrounding towns. But the rollers between valley towns accumulate fatigue on already-taxed legs. This is the mental crux: the big climb is behind you, but 40+ km of riding remains with no extended flat recovery.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Descending mountain road transitioning to rolling valley roads, scenic river valley terrain

suffering_zones.label: Return to Cordoba
suffering_zones.desc: The final 25 km uses the Autovia Punilla and Cordoba-Carlos Paz highway to return to the city. This is the grinding finale -- largely flat to gently rolling, but after 100+ km and 1,800+ meters of climbing, the legs are heavy. Headwinds are possible on the open highway sections. The approach to Kempes Stadium reignites the final push. The Tour de France finish-line experience at the stadium provides the emotional fuel for the last kilometers.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Highway and main road, flat to gently rolling, return to 400m city elevation

course_description.signature_challenge: The Camino del Cuadrado -- the mountain pass connecting the Sierras Chicas to the Punilla Valley. Rising approximately 800m with sustained steep gradients on recently repaved mountain road, this climb determines the race. The combination of the concentrated ascent followed by the grinding return leg across the valley and back to Cordoba creates a course that punishes riders who go too deep on the big climb and have nothing left for the final hour.

guide_variables.race_name: L'Etape Argentina by Tour de France
guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved mountain and valley roads -- fully closed course through the Sierras de Cordoba and Punilla Valley
guide_variables.race_weather: Warm late-summer conditions. 20-27C (68-81F) during the day. Moderate humidity. Low afternoon thunderstorm risk.
race_challenges: 2,067m of climbing with the Camino del Cuadrado as the decisive ascent
Sustained steep gradients (8-12%) on the mountain pass section
Grinding return leg after the main climbing block -- 40+ km of fatigue management
Warm temperatures requiring consistent hydration and fueling over 5-8 hours

non_negotiables.requirement: Mountain climbing endurance -- sustained 30-50 minute efforts at threshold on steep gradients
non_negotiables.why: The Camino del Cuadrado is a proper mountain pass with 800m of ascending and gradients that regularly hit 8-12%. You need to sustain power output on steep pitches for an extended period. Train with repeated climbs of 20-40 minutes at tempo-to-threshold to simulate the sustained effort required. If your training is flat, this climb will break you.

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat and hydration management over 5-8 hours
non_negotiables.why: Late March in Cordoba means 25-27C temperatures at lower elevations. Over 134 km and 5-8 hours of riding, dehydration and calorie deficit are the most common reasons for bonking on the return leg. Practice your fueling strategy on long rides in warm conditions. Target 500-750ml of fluid per hour and 60-90g of carbohydrates per hour. The feed zones will help, but you need to carry and consume consistently between them.

non_negotiables.requirement: Pacing discipline -- manage the Camino del Cuadrado so you have legs for the final 50 km
non_negotiables.why: The natural temptation is to race the signature climb. But the Camino del Cuadrado sits roughly at the halfway point of the course, and you still have 50+ km including the return to Cordoba. Going into oxygen debt on the climb and then facing the grinding return on empty legs is the classic L'Etape mistake. Practice negative-split climbing: start conservative, finish strong.
